video_title: Things To Fall Apart After The Mid-Terms? | Stephanie Pomboy
gist: Macro analyst Stephanie Pomboy argues nothing material breaks before the US midterms, but expects credit cracks to appear after November as oil re-rises and K-shaped consumer stress deepens. She is a long-term energy and hard-asset bull (adding oil, gold miners, rare earths), views the AI complex as a bubble whose most dangerous pin is a capex write-down, and sees corporate America shifting from buyback king to net borrower competing with the Treasury for capital.

insights.claim: AI-trade leadership has rotated out of the Mag 7 hyperscalers (which have lagged over recent months) and into semiconductors, which 'went white hot,' narrowing the rally into a single highly cyclical sector vulnerable to a downturn.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Watch semis as the load-bearing leg of the index; a cyclical rollover there removes the offset that has masked Mag 7 weakness and could pull the whole market down.

insights.claim: The hyperscalers have burned through their free cash flow and shifted from being cash cows to tapping the credit markets to fund AI capex, turning former self-funders into external borrowers.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Rising AI-related debt issuance adds supply to credit markets and makes the buildout hostage to financing conditions.

insights.claim: Employment in AI-related industries has begun to decline even as capex is being spent, which Pomboy reads as a possible signal that order books are drying up and firms are pre-adjusting to a slowdown.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Falling AI-sector hiring could foreshadow weaker payrolls, since AI, education and healthcare have been the main job contributors.

insights.claim: Companies that tried to replace employees with AI/automation are now saying they want humans back, indicating the AI-labor-substitution trade 'jumped the shark' and firms are backpedaling on productivity assumptions.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: If AI's near-term productivity payoff disappoints, ROI on capex gets questioned and spending plans could be cut.

insights.claim: The single most dangerous 'pin' for the AI bubble is whatever event forces sell-side analysts to write down their expected forward capex projections, because AI-linked stocks are so pervasively owned that a markdown would hit the top decile of consumers hardest.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Track analyst capex revisions as the key trigger; a downward revision cascade could crater the most over-owned stocks and change wealthy-consumer behavior.
insights.quote: the one that really matters is whichever one forces analysts to start writing down their expected capex projections going forward

insights.claim: Physical real-world constraints, not funding, may cap the AI data-center buildout: shortages of capacitors, permitted land, water, copper and skilled engineers mean centers cannot be built as fast as spreadsheets assume, per Jesse Felder's argument corroborated by on-the-ground engineer feedback.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Bottlenecks in copper, power and construction labor are a real-economy brake on the capex boom and a potential catalyst for a downward revision.

insights.claim: Pomboy frames AI as a classic technology-infrastructure bubble where participants extrapolate current growth infinitely and overbuild, because in a new-infrastructure buildout you cannot see the line between 'not enough' and 'too much' until you have already passed it.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: History says the top forms exactly when the '10 more years of growth' consensus peaks; use overbuild dynamics to time the eventual AI capex bust.
insights.quote: you don't know where the line between not enough and too much is

insights.claim: Corporate America is shifting from repurchasing over $1 trillion a year in shares to becoming a net issuer and net borrower, reversing the buyback tailwind that boosted both prices and EPS.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: An expanding equity supply plus lost EPS flattery removes a structural bid that has propped up valuations for years.

insights.claim: Formerly cash-rich, high-quality companies coming to raise capital will compete with a federal government that must roll roughly $10 trillion in T-bills and related paper this year, a competition for capital Pomboy expects to eventually 'get ugly.'
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Crowding-out between corporates and Treasury supply is a setup for higher yields and credit-market dislocations.

insights.claim: Pomboy expects any yield stress to show up first in low-quality corporate paper, as issuers are forced to pay up to attract buyers amid heavy supply.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Monitor low-quality corporate spreads as the first canary of the capital-competition thesis.

insights.claim: Private-equity-owned portfolio companies (10,000+ names) that aren't generating adequate income are undergoing 'liability management exercises' that Pomboy characterizes as a glorified default and restructure, and this will accelerate unless rates fall.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Rising interest expense is quietly forcing PE restructurings; a stall in rate cuts accelerates defaults across private credit.
insights.quote: a glorified default and then restructure

insights.claim: The BDC (business development company) index never caught a bid even during the market melt-up and continues to trend lower, which Pomboy reads as a persistent warning signal from the private-credit complex.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: BDC weakness diverging from a rising market is an under-followed tell that private credit stress is real, not resolved.

insights.claim: Private credit is a fire 'simmering beneath view' being deftly managed for now, with funds like Blue Owl having put up gates and books being sold between big firms in a 'hot potato' that has not yet burned anyone.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Gating and book transfers suggest suppressed rather than resolved stress; a break here becomes systemic if the consumer credit cycle turns.

insights.claim: New Fed chair Kevin Warsh's stated plan is to cut interest rates while simultaneously shrinking the balance sheet, the 180-degree opposite of Pomboy's long-held view that the balance sheet, not the funds rate, is now the true tool of monetary accommodation.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: If Pomboy is right, watch balance-sheet policy over the funds rate for the real accommodation signal; Warsh's framework could clash with market plumbing.
insights.quote: the balance sheet would be the tool of monetary accommodation and the funds rate is basically irrelevant anymore

insights.claim: Markets read Warsh's post-FOMC press conference as hawkish, an 'anti-Draghi' willing to raise rates as high as needed to kill inflation, though the Fed did not actually hike and the tone was likely softened by the US-Iran de-escalation and falling oil.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: A credibly hawkish Fed chair is a headwind for gold and long duration until credit stress forces a pivot.

insights.claim: Both Pomboy and Taggart judge the Fed's next move more likely to be a cut than a hike, because eventual credit stress will force the Fed to come in and ease despite current hawkish rhetoric.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Positioning for an eventual dovish pivot on credit stress favors rate-sensitive and hard-asset trades over betting on hikes.

insights.claim: Pomboy is a long-term energy bull who expects oil to normalize from the Iran spike toward $65-67 and then edge structurally higher over the next several years, calling the current pullback a tremendous buying opportunity she intends to add to.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: A secular-higher oil view argues for accumulating energy exposure on the post-ceasefire dip rather than trading the near-term decline.

insights.claim: Strategic petroleum reserves were heavily depleted globally during the oil-price spike (US SPR back to post-Biden-release lows), creating a large future replenishment demand that Pomboy expects to begin after the midterms and to support prices.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Post-midterm SPR refilling is a structural demand pillar under oil that most models ignore.

insights.claim: Countries that lacked strategic energy reserves (Australia, India, Japan, Korea) are likely to build them post-crisis, while Norway has announced it will withhold oil exports to rebuild its own stockpile, tightening global supply as producers husband barrels.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: A wave of new sovereign reserve-building diverts exportable barrels and adds a demand shock the market underprices.

insights.claim: Commodities analyst Jeff Curry is bullish oil and plans to express it through producers because he believes they are priced for oil to stay at $50-60 for a long time, a scenario he rejects; producer equities have sold off heavily in the drawdown.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: If oil re-rates higher, beaten-down producers priced for $50-60 crude offer more leverage than the commodity itself.

insights.claim: Pomboy views the risk of a windfall-profits tax on energy producers as more likely to come from a Democratic administration than from Trump, whom she sees as unlikely to shake down 'drill baby drill' producers despite his public jawboning of gas prices.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Headline risk on producers is real but policy-tail risk is lower under the current administration, supporting the producer long thesis.

insights.claim: US consumer credit-card delinquencies are the highest since the 2008 GFC and subprime auto-loan delinquencies are at an all-time record, credit deterioration that Pomboy notes actually fits the weak consumer-sentiment readings.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Record consumer delinquencies are the hard-data tell that the bottom of the K is fraying beneath the surface.

insights.claim: Consumer-sentiment surveys have lost their value as leading indicators and diverge from spending, but Pomboy argues the direction (still falling when it should be rising) plus the notable drop in Republican sentiment is the meaningful signal.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Read sentiment for direction and partisan composition, not absolute level; a Republican-led drop under Trump is a genuine warning.

insights.claim: The share of consumer spending going to non-discretionary items (insurance, healthcare, housing) is near the highest ever, explaining weak sentiment even as headline spending holds up, with rising cash-out refis and HELOC use repeating pre-2008 behavior.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Consumers leaning on home equity and cards to fund essentials signals a stretched household balance sheet vulnerable to a credit turn.

insights.claim: The paused student-loan wage garnishment (quietly halted by the administration early this year) is likely to resume after the midterms, adding a fresh cash-flow stress to already-strained bottom-of-the-K consumers.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Resumed garnishment is a post-midterm demand headwind and delinquency accelerant for lower-income borrowers.

insights.claim: Both hosts agree the weak bottom leg of the K-shaped economy does not matter to markets or GDP until it becomes a credit problem large enough to ricochet through the financial vehicles in which that debt is packaged.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: The signal to de-risk is when lower-end credit deterioration starts impairing securitized/packaged debt, not when sentiment sours.

insights.claim: Pomboy's best guess is that nothing material breaks before the US midterms, but cracks in the credit system likely emerge anytime after November as oil re-rises and consumers face both bill stress and higher costs of essentials.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Positions the post-November window as the key risk period; 'all bets are off after November' when the economy is let to do what it will.

insights.claim: Pomboy worries more about the top of the K as the trigger for a credit event: markets priced for perfection on AI and rates are set up for disappointment, and a resulting risk-off would hit wealthy portfolios first, then cause layoffs that impair bottom-of-K credit quality.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: The dangerous sequence starts with a top-of-K asset drawdown, not a bottom-up consumer default wave; watch equity risk-off as the initiating shock.

insights.claim: Pomboy flags housing as an alternate credit trigger: heavy new inventory is arriving while mortgage rates have not fallen enough to restore affordability, so price becomes the release valve, threatening net worth and mortgage credit quality.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: A supply-driven home-price adjustment could hit both wealth and mortgage credit, opening a second front for the credit bubble.

insights.claim: Taggart remains, on the margin, bullish on 2026 economic growth provided the AI capex keeps flowing and oil keeps falling, but names the AI complex as the single biggest candidate to derail that base case.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: The bull case is explicitly contingent on continued AI spending; that spending is also the biggest identified tail risk.

insights.claim: Pomboy sees a secular rotation underway from paper assets to hard assets, evidenced by central banks and sophisticated investors preferring hard assets given the unsustainable fiscal situation across the G7.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: A multi-year paper-to-hard-asset shift is the overarching allocation thesis favoring commodities, gold and strategic resources.
insights.quote: we're now in sort of a secular rotation from paper to hard assets

insights.claim: Pomboy has held through a roughly 25% pullback in precious metals and a steeper drop in the miners, treating it as a standard correction within a long-term bull market and planning to add miners and gold exposure rather than trim.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: High conviction to buy the metals dip signals the pullback is viewed as noise; miners offer the leveraged expression.

insights.claim: Gold sold off partly because central banks liquidated gold to raise dollars to pay for higher-priced oil during the Iran conflict, and Pomboy expects those reserves to be rebuilt just like SPRs, restoring a bid under the metal.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: The oil-driven gold selling is reversible; central-bank reserve rebuilding is a forward catalyst for gold as oil stress fades.

insights.claim: Pomboy is broadening into strategic resources including rare earths (a move she says has already missed part of the run) on the pullback, citing a global 'cold war for resources' as a structural bid under commodities.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: Diversifying hard-asset exposure into rare earths and strategic materials plays a geopolitical resource-competition tailwind.
insights.quote: I think there's a a global uh cold war for resources that's underway

insights.claim: Taggart says he is seriously considering putting 'gold laser eyes' on, waiting mainly for a possible rollover in the US dollar, and argues the original 2025 gold thesis is unchanged and has gotten better while oil-driven and Warsh-hawkish headwinds fade.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: A dollar rollover is the missing catalyst Taggart is watching to turn outright bullish gold; tailwinds are rebuilding as oil and Fed pressures ease.

insights.claim: Pomboy frames the US demographic problem as an already-emerging fiscal problem for Social Security, with more people set to take out than pay in, arguing the country genuinely needs real immigration even as the birthright-citizenship system needs reform.
insights.speaker: Stephanie Pomboy
insights.so_what: The demographic-fiscal squeeze underpins the long-term deficit and hard-asset thesis and shapes immigration-policy risk.

insights.claim: Both hosts advocate using the tax code to incentivize domestic family formation (e.g., a first-time homebuyer credit tied to having a child), on the Charlie Munger view that incentives drive behavior, as a partial fix for the demographic/fiscal backdrop.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Pro-natalist tax incentives are a policy theme investors can watch for downstream effects on housing and consumer sectors.
